result.chapter_summary: Chapter 5 argues that composition should be taught as an autonomous craft rather than being subsumed under subjects like literature or logic. The author contends that the pedagogical assumptions of literary study (fusion of form and content) conflict with those of composition (separation of form and content), and that 'clear thinking' in logic is distinct from 'clear writing' in prose.

claims.text: Composition is a craft that cannot properly be subsumed under any conventional subject matter, such as literature, logic, or rhetoric.
claims.source_passage: Everything I have learned from my researches points toward the correctness of the second point of view—that composition is a craft which cannot properly be subsumed under any conventional subject matter. It is not a branch of literary study, or logic, or even of rhetoric.

claims.text: Stressing instruction in literature, logic, or rhetoric within a writing course is an inefficient way of teaching composition.
claims.source_passage: Even in composition courses, there are good reasons for giving instruction in those three fields. But to stress such instruction is a very inefficient way of teaching composition.

claims.text: The desire to mix composition with other instructional goals stems from a lack of knowledge regarding effective ways to teach composition.
claims.source_passage: I have become convinced that one reason for the desire to mix composition with other instructional goals is the ignorance that besets us all about effective ways to teach composition.

claims.text: The Renaissance humanists considered the attainment of a good prose style to be a primary goal of culture.
claims.source_passage: Yet the humanists of the Renaissance thought that a good prose style was a chief goal of culture, and a sound case can be made for their view.

claims.text: Knowing how to write is distinct and different from knowing about literature.
claims.source_passage: First, it can be shown that knowing how to write is different from knowing about literature.

claims.text: The presence of graduate students in literature who cannot write well proves that knowledge of literature does not equate to writing skill.
claims.source_passage: Numbers of graduate students in literature are unable to write well, yet they do demonstrably know a great deal about literature, much more than a freshman could possibly learn in a composition course.

claims.text: The teaching of writing and the teaching of literature involve conflicting assumptions regarding the nature of style.
claims.source_passage: There is, moreover, a strong reason for separating the teaching of writing from the teaching of literature. While both subjects may make students aware of style, they do so in conflicting ways.

claims.text: Literary study assumes the fusion of form with content, whereas composition assumes the separation of linguistic form and content.
claims.source_passage: The study of style in literature is a study of the fusion of form with content. But learning how to write implies just the opposite assumption; it assumes the separation of linguistic form and content.

claims.text: Learning the craft of prose consists of learning to express the same meaning in a different and more effective way.
claims.source_passage: Learning the craft of prose is learning to write the same meaning in a different and more effective way.

claims.text: There is a logical flaw in equating 'clear thinking' with 'clear writing' because the word 'clear' has different meanings in each context.
claims.source_passage: There is, in fact, a logical flaw in equating clear thinking and clear writing. The word clear means something different in the two phrases.

claims.text: Clear thinking consists of drawing correct inferences from given premises.
claims.source_passage: Clear thinking means drawing correct inferences from the given premises.

claims.text: Clear writing consists of an unambiguous and readable expression of one’s meaning.
claims.source_passage: Clear writing means an unambiguous and readable expression of one’s meaning.

claims.text: Instruction in formal logic is an inefficient and largely wasteful way to provide instruction in writing.
claims.source_passage: I believe, as a practical matter, that instruction in logic is a very inefficient way to give instruction in writing. It is largely, I think, a waste of valuable time.

claims.text: Muddy writing can express clear thinking, and clear writing can express muddy thinking.
claims.source_passage: I have argued, and have illustrated the point, that muddy writing can express clear thinking and that clear writing can express muddy thinking.

claims.text: Logical students often write badly because they fail to make explicit the logical connections in their arguments.
claims.source_passage: Highly logical and clear-headed students often write badly because they fail to make explicit some of the implicit logical connections in their argument.

claims.text: There is often a connection between the quality of writing and the quality of thought.
claims.source_passage: I wish therefore to concede the point that there is often a connection between quality of writing and of thought, whether or not it is a necessary connection.

claims.text: Teaching fallacy-lists and truth tables in a writing class is a waste of valuable time.
claims.source_passage: It is largely, I think, a waste of valuable time. So long as fallacy-lists and truth tables are left outside the writing class, it does not matter—except perhaps in grading policy—whether the teacher is a separatist.

claims.text: The efficiency of logic instruction for teaching clear writing is an empirical question that could be definitively answered by research.
claims.source_passage: Moreover, the practical question of whether logic instruction is an efficient way to teach “clear” writing is a question that could be definitively answered. One good piece of empirical research would yield the answer.

claims.text: Most speech, both oral and written, contains a strong rhetorical element because authors must account for their audience.
claims.source_passage: And there is a strong rhetorical element in writing, since an author is normally concerned with the effect of his text on a reader and must, like the rhetorician, take account of his audience. One can even say, on those grounds, that there is a strong rhetorical element in most speech, whether oral or written.

claims.text: Linguistics and rhetoric are not coextensive with composition and contain many concerns irrelevant to it.
claims.source_passage: But that is part of the trouble with rhetoric and linguistics. They are not coextensive with composition. Many of their concerns are as irrelevant to composition as the concerns of literature and logic.

claims.text: Competent writing teachers have recently abandoned instruction in linguistic concepts and terms.
claims.source_passage: there is a lot of evidence to suggest that sensible writing teachers have recently abandoned instruction in the concepts and terms of linguistics.

claims.text: Writing can be well-executed even when it is indifferent to emotive or persuasive rhetorical effects.
claims.source_passage: A legal statute may be well or badly written, while remaining indifferent to its emotive or persuasive effects on readers.

claims.text: Attempts to subsume composition under traditional academic subjects rest on the false assumption that composition is a humanistic subject matter rather than a practical art.
claims.source_passage: The attempts to subsume composition under conventional university subjects like logic, literature, linguistics, and rhetoric, have in common the hidden assumption that composition must come under some humanistic subject matter.

claims.text: Learning to write is the acquisition of practical knowledge, not theoretical knowledge.
claims.source_passage: To learn how to write is to gain practical, not theoretical, knowledge.

claims.text: Writing should be taught as a practical subject, emphasizing student production and teacher commentary.
claims.source_passage: Writing needs to be taught as a practical, not a theoretical, subject. The teacher of drawing asks his students to make drawings, and then comments on what his students have produced. That is the basic character of teaching in all the practical arts, including the art of writing.

claims.text: Composition is a branch of practical knowledge in its own right and has an importance equal to any other university subject except reading.
claims.source_passage: It is not part of another subject matter but a branch of practical knowledge in its own right. In relation to our culture as a whole, it is a kind of knowledge that has an importance equal to any other subject in the curriculum, except reading.

claims.text: Herbert Spencer's attempt to reduce stylistic maxims to the principle of economy was successful but practically difficult due to its generality.
claims.source_passage: Herbert Spencer’s attempt to reduce all stylistic maxims to a single principle was in fact a successful attempt. But the practical difficulty with Spencer’s principle of economy is its generality.

claims.text: Spencer’s principle of economy fails to provide students with practical help for making concrete stylistic decisions.
claims.source_passage: But his principle does not tell us how to judge either psychological economy or sameness of purpose in particular cases. Spencer simplified the problem of style by abstracting it, but then omitted to give the student practical help in making concrete decisions.

claims.text: No single stylistic maxim, such as 'use concrete rather than abstract words,' holds true for all writing cases.
claims.source_passage: Probably no single maxim of composition holds for all cases. If a student is told “use concrete rather than abstract words,” that maxim will fail him in a good many instances.

claims.text: A long, complex sentence or a multisyllabic word can be more economical than a short, simple one depending on the particular semantic intention.
claims.source_passage: thus, magnificent can be better than grand, and a long, complex sentence can be better than a short, simple one.

claims.text: Spencer's universal imperative dictates choosing the most economical expression from different expressions that serve the same purpose.
claims.source_passage: His universal imperative is simply that we should choose the most economical expression from among those different expressions which serve the same purpose.

claims.text: Spencer's principle fails to provide practical criteria for judging psychological economy or identity of purpose in specific writing situations.
claims.source_passage: But his principle does not tell us how to judge either psychological economy or sameness of purpose in particular cases. Spencer simplified the problem of style by abstracting it, but then omitted to give the student practical help in making concrete decisions.

claims.text: Practical maxims of composition are inherently problematic because a large number of them confuses students, while a small number misleads them.
claims.source_passage: But “practical” maxims have their own problems. If they are many they can confuse the student, and if they are few they can mislead him.

claims.text: Practical maxims frequently conflict with one another in application, such as the tension between using concrete words and using appropriate words.
claims.source_passage: And if the student is also instructed to “use appropriate words,” the two maxims will sometimes be in conflict.

claims.text: A general principle is often more practical than a specific maxim because the principle is always true, whereas maxims require difficult situational judgment.
claims.source_passage: Because of this exigency, a general principle (being always true) is often more practical than a directly practical maxim. To place confidence in maxims alone is to lean on a weak reed.

claims.text: Maxims are indispensable devices for teaching the practical art of writing.
claims.source_passage: But experience suggests that maxims are indispensable devices in teaching any practical art.

claims.text: It is possible to distinguish more important maxims from less important ones based on how quickly they help students achieve basic writing proficiency.
claims.source_passage: If our aim is to teach writing more efficiently, then we may find it useful to distinguish the more important maxims from the less important ones. It would be useful to know, for instance, which set of maxims will normally help the student achieve basic writing proficiency in the shortest time.

claims.text: Established maxims of composition correlate significantly with the psychological principles of readability.
claims.source_passage: For instance, it is important for me to show, in documenting my theoretical case, that the tried and true maxims of composition turn out to correlate very well with the psychological principles of readability.

claims.text: The utility of a specific maxim is relative to the individual student's specific deficiencies.
claims.source_passage: No rating of maxims can be altogether reliable, since different students will have different deficiencies—a consideration which points again to the superior utility of general principles over practical maxims.

claims.text: The stylistic injunctions found in modern composition handbooks are remarkably similar to those used in Herbert Spencer's era.
claims.source_passage: The handbooks are still saying what they said in Herbert Spencer’s day and before: use familiar, concrete words; keep related words together; and so on.

claims.text: Different composition handbooks often express the same underlying stylistic advice through different phrasing.
claims.source_passage: A number of the injunctions listed above are saying very similar things in different ways. For instance, where Strunk says “Choose a suitable design and stick to it,” we find in Crews “Keep to your point,” in McCrimmon “choose and restrict your subject,” and in Gowers “Know what you want to say.”

claims.text: The content and emphasis of composition maxims are determined by the specific failings and needs of the intended audience.
claims.source_passage: Since his audience was different from the audience for college handbooks, so were his composition maxims different from those addressed to freshmen.

claims.text: Sir Ernest Gowers's stylistic advice focused on brevity and humanity because his audience of government bureaucrats had already mastered technical elements like coordination and transitions.
claims.source_passage: Gowers assumed that the bureaucrat he addressed knew how to spell, punctuate, and form paragraphs. Since his book was aimed at the characteristic failings of bureaucratic writing, he chose not to discuss coordination, transitions, or even sentence variety.

claims.text: It is impossible to rank stylistic maxims in order of absolute importance without considering the specific learners and the genres they are writing in.
claims.source_passage: You cannot list them in the order of their importance unless you take into account the learners to whom the maxims are addressed, and unless you also consider the chief kinds of writing which those learners are undertaking to learn.

claims.text: The pedagogical value of any specific stylistic maxim is relative to the writing context.
claims.source_passage: But if it is true that the pedagogical value of a maxim will vary with the audience and the genre of writing, then it must follow that no single list of maxims will serve all purposes.

claims.text: Composition cannot be taught effectively through lists of maxims alone.
claims.source_passage: And that is another way of saying that we cannot depend upon maxims alone in the teaching of composition.

claims.text: Effective composition instruction must include the genuine psycholinguistic and linguistic principles that underlie stylistic maxims.
claims.source_passage: We need to teach genuine principles of composition as well—the principles behind the maxims.

claims.text: All proficient writers have internalised the underlying principles of composition, regardless of whether they studied formal handbooks.
claims.source_passage: That is what every good writer has learned, with or without the aid of the handbooks.

claims.text: Every genre of prose exhibits significant variety and overlap with other genres.
claims.source_passage: But, in fact, every genre of prose exhibits much variety, and also a lot of overlap with other genres.

claims.text: No prose genre, including technical and scientific writing, possesses watertight conventions.
claims.source_passage: No genre, not even the technical, scientific article, has watertight conventions.

claims.text: Writing proficiency is a general skill that is transferable across many different genres.
claims.source_passage: He possesses a general proficiency that is transferable to many different genres.

claims.text: The principles underlying stylistic maxims are general principles applicable to all genres of prose.
claims.source_passage: That is what one would expect if the principles behind the maxims were general principles, applicable to all genres of prose.

claims.text: Writing is taught more efficiently when teachers understand the psychological principles behind stylistic maxims.
claims.source_passage: But it might be the case that writing would be taught more efficiently if the teachers knew the principles behind the maxims.

claims.text: Every concrete rule of style is open to exceptions.
claims.source_passage: Yet every concrete rule of style is open to exceptions, even the rule 'Omit needless words.'

claims.text: Inexperienced writers often supply too little explicit context, causing reader uncertainty.
claims.source_passage: Inexperienced writers are often too brief. They supply too little explicit context, and thereby unintentionally leave the reader with too many uncertainties.

claims.text: Stylistic maxims are most accurately understood as conditional instructions rather than absolute rules.
claims.source_passage: The accurate form of all such maxims is: 'Do X, unless you have weighty reasons for not doing X.'

claims.text: Positive statements are psychologically more linear and therefore understood more rapidly than negative ones.
claims.source_passage: Strunk knew by experience what psycholinguists have now established by experiment, namely, that we understand positive statements more rapidly than negative ones, because positives are psychologically more linear than negatives.

claims.text: The process of understanding a negative statement involves first understanding its positive sense and then negating it.
claims.source_passage: With a negative we normally first understand its positive sense, and then we take the second step of negating this positive sense.

claims.text: Meaning is taken up more securely from concrete words than from abstract words.
claims.source_passage: There is good evidence from recent experiments that we do take up meaning more securely from concrete than from abstract words.

claims.text: Sacrificing readability is a worthwhile trade-off if it serves the goal of accurate communication.
claims.source_passage: The sacrifice is worthwhile if it serves accurate communication.

claims.text: The active voice is usually easier for the human mind to process than the passive voice.
claims.source_passage: And indeed, the active voice is usually easier to process than the passive.

claims.text: Keeping related words together in a sentence facilitates more rapid semantic closure.
claims.source_passage: To keep related words together does effect more rapid semantic closure than to separate them.

claims.text: Stylistic rules in writing are primarily 'rules of thumb' that contain an implicit 'unless' clause based on the writer's specific purpose.
claims.source_passage: Stylistic rules always imply an unless clause: “Use the familiar word, unless the rare one suits your purpose better.” Use the concrete word, unless the abstract one works better.”

claims.text: Breaking a stylistic rule usually results in a sacrifice of readability.
claims.source_passage: What the student needs to know, and what every practiced writer does know, is that he usually sacrifices some degree of readability when he breaks the rule.

claims.text: Unambiguous word order reduces the cognitive processing time required by the reader.
claims.source_passage: And using an unambiguous word order does cut down processing time for the reader.

claims.text: The injunction to vary sentence structure is a nearly universal maxim because monotony in writing is indefensible.
claims.source_passage: The injunction to vary sentence structure must come as close to universality as any maxim of writing. Being boring is hard to defend on any grounds.

claims.text: The paragraph functions as the primary unit of composition because it aligns with the universal human attention mechanism.
claims.source_passage: There is also a sound psychological reason for stressing the paragraph as the unit of composition... The universality of the rule arises from the universality of the attention mechanism.

claims.text: The human attention mechanism is governed by the principle of focusing on one thing at a time.
claims.source_passage: One-thing-at-a-time is its governing principle, and its corollary in writing is one-theme-at-a-time.

claims.text: A theme must achieve a degree of semantic closure before it can be effectively stored as an element in long-term memory.
claims.source_passage: A theme must have some degree of semantic closure before it can function as a textual element in long-term memory.

claims.text: The rule to 'choose a design and stick to it' is necessary in writing because, unlike oral speech, written discourse must provide its own context.
claims.source_passage: In written discourse, you have to choose a design and stick to it, because the discourse itself is the chief context of the utterance.

claims.text: Changing a design mid-stream in a text creates uncertainty for the reader by shifting the established context.
claims.source_passage: To change the design is to change the context and therefore to throw into uncertainty what the reader thought he was understanding.

claims.text: Using parallel forms for similar ideas increases processing speed by exploiting the principle of short-term expectancy.
claims.source_passage: The use of parallel forms to present similar ideas will speed up understanding by exploiting the principle of short-term expectancy.

claims.text: A hallmark of readable prose is that the reader's initial hypothesis about the meaning of a sentence turns out to be correct.
claims.source_passage: When we read writing that has followed this rule of parallel form, our first meaning-hypothesis will tend to be the right one, and this is a crucial feature of readable prose.

claims.text: Composition handbooks overemphasize large-scale outlines while neglecting short-term devices of semantic integration.
claims.source_passage: Where the handbooks emphasize large-scale organization and the writing of outlines, they might better stress shorter-term devices of semantic integration.

claims.text: Short-term devices of expectation-fulfillment are more essential to prose quality than explicit written outlines.
claims.source_passage: While written outlines are highly useful... they are not absolutely essential devices for choosing a design and sticking to it. On the other hand, good prose cannot dispense with short-term devices of expectation-fulfillment.

claims.text: Proleptic words (e.g., 'however', 'therefore') are more necessary in written speech than in oral speech because they compensate for the lack of intonation and situational clues.
claims.source_passage: One such integrative device, far more essential to writing than to oral speech, is the proleptic word or phrase... Proleptics thereby speed up understanding and integrate meaning, despite the absence of intonation- and situation-clues which perform these two functions in oral speech.

claims.text: Proleptic devices perform the dual function of constraining the meaning of the current clause and integrating it with previous clauses.
claims.source_passage: The rule is important because proleptic devices serve the double function of constraining the meaning of the ongoing clause and integrating that meaning with earlier ones.

claims.text: Proleptic words or phrases are integrative devices that are far more essential to writing than to oral speech.
claims.source_passage: One such integrative device, far more essential to writing than to oral speech, is the proleptic word or phrase: but, similarly, also, on the other hand, moreover, by contrast, nonetheless, likewise, therefore, however, and so on.

claims.text: Thematic tags, involving the repetition of words or phrases across clauses, are a universal and essential integrative device in good writing.
claims.source_passage: Another integrative device of equal importance and universality is the thematic tag. A tag word or phrase in a preceding clause should be repeated in the one that follows... the principle of repetition is unavoidable in good writing.

claims.text: Human memory rapidly forgets specific linguistic forms, such as particular lexical and syntactic structures, after a few sentences.
claims.source_passage: Only recently have psychological researchers discovered the previously unsuspected fact that we rapidly forget linguistic forms.

claims.text: Thematic tags serve their primary function in closely adjoining sentences before the reader's lexical memory decays.
claims.source_passage: The repeated tags serve their principal function across closely adjoining sentences, where they can integrate a sequence of meanings before lexical memory has begun to decay.

claims.text: Middle-range integrative techniques are likely the most important writing techniques to be taught because they bridge the gap between individual sentences and discourse.
claims.source_passage: But, in fact, the integrative, middle-range techniques of writing are probably the most important techniques to be taught. They bridge the gap between readable single sentences and readable discourse.

claims.text: The psychological principle of 'economy of attention' is the governing principle of effective written expression.
claims.source_passage: The discussion has shown that Herbert Spencer’s insight was a sound one, and that psychological economy is the governing principle which determines the most effective written expression of one’s intended meaning.

claims.text: Omitting needless words and keeping related words together ensures rapid semantic closure and prevents taxing the reader's short-term memory.
claims.source_passage: These two rules assure rapid semantic closure when the reader is processing a clause. If the writer omits extensive qualifications between a noun and verb, for instance, the reader can quickly achieve semantic closure without taxing his short-term memory.

claims.text: Sentence length is merely an indirect indicator of readability, not its primary cause.
claims.source_passage: The gain in readability comes only indirectly from converting one long sentence into three short ones. Sentence length is at best an indirect indication of readability.

claims.text: Channeling the reader's attention constrains their meaning-guesses and facilitates the integration of understood meanings.
claims.source_passage: When the reader’s attention is channeled, his meaning-guesses will also be constrained, and his understood meanings will therefore tend to be integrated with one another.

claims.text: The gain in readability from converting one long sentence into several short ones is indirect.
claims.source_passage: he gain in readability comes only indirectly from converting one long sentence into three short ones.

claims.text: The primary reasons to break up long sentences are to keep related words together and omit needless intervening words.
claims.source_passage: My main reasons for breaking up the long sentence were to keep related words together and to omit needless intervening words.

claims.text: The most important rules for prose, after memory-management rules, are those that channel reader attention, constrain meaning-guesses, and integrate understanding.
claims.source_passage: if we turn from these two rules which mainly ease the burden on short-term memory, we will find that the most important remaining rules are those which channel the reader’s attention, constrain his meaning-guesses, and integrate his understanding.

claims.text: When a reader's attention is channeled, their meaning-guesses are automatically constrained and their understood meanings tend to be integrated.
claims.source_passage: When the reader’s attention is channeled, his meaning-guesses will also be constrained, and his understood meanings will therefore tend to be integrated with one another.

claims.text: Making the paragraph the unit of composition constrains the reader’s semantic expectations on a large scale.
claims.source_passage: The first maxim would constrain the reader’s semantic expectations on the large scale, and therefore economize his efforts to understand the gist of the text.

claims.text: Using integrative devices between clauses and sentences economizes the reader’s processing effort in the middle range of comprehension.
claims.source_passage: The second maxim would constrain and economize the reader’s effort in the middle range between his clause processing and his overall understanding.

claims.text: A reader cannot understand the meaning of a clause sequence until they understand its main theme.
claims.source_passage: The reader cannot understand the meaning of a clause sequence until he understands its main theme.

claims.text: Themes in writing must be expressed by verbal means because they cannot be found in a concrete, nonverbal situation.
claims.source_passage: This theme has to be expressed by verbal means, since it cannot be found in a concrete, nonverbal situation.

claims.text: Writing in paragraphs implies an explicit focus on a theme long enough to contextualize and constrain the reader's meaning-expectations.
claims.source_passage: To write in paragraphs therefore implies an explicit focus on a theme for a long enough time to contextualize and constrain the clauses which convey the writer’s meaning.

claims.text: Adjacent repetition is the key element in small-scale devices that unify a paragraph.
claims.source_passage: The key element in all such integration devices is the element of adjacent repetition.

claims.text: Using parallel forms in adjacent phrases fulfills reader expectations by repeating a form.
claims.source_passage: To use parallel forms in adjacent phrases and clauses is to repeat a form and therefore fulfill an expectation.

claims.text: Repeating a syntactic form from a distant sentence is ineffective because the reader will not remember the earlier form.
claims.source_passage: (But it is pointless to repeat a syntactic form from a distant sentence, since the reader will not dependably remember the earlier form.)

claims.text: Proleptic words like 'however' function as a form of adjacent repetition.
claims.source_passage: Even the use of proleptic words like however, is a kind of adjacent repetition, since the qualification predicted by the however requires a remembering and therefore an implicit repetition of the adjacent meaning that is being qualified.

claims.text: The conscious use of adjacent repetitions indirectly combats monotony by forcing the writer to vary syntax and sentence length.
claims.source_passage: Repetition indirectly combats monotony. For, paradoxically, the use of integrative repetitions in nearby phrases will force variations on the writer by compelling him to rearrange his word sequences.

claims.text: Reliable evaluation of whether specific maxims are efficient for teaching requires further reliable research.
claims.source_passage: Reliable evaluation of such predictions will have to await reliable research.

claims.text: The author's proposed maxims for composition may not prove to be the most efficient tools for teaching, despite their generality and power.
claims.source_passage: I am not suggesting that they will prove to be the most efficient maxims for teaching composition.

claims.text: Current predictions about the efficient use of pedagogical maxims are speculative because of the complexity of the composing and learning processes.
claims.source_passage: But so many factors are at work in the composing process, and also in the learning process, that any present prediction about the efficient use of maxims is just a stab in the dark.

claims.text: Reliable evaluation of teaching methods requires reliable research that has not yet been conducted.
claims.source_passage: Reliable evaluation of such predictions will have to await reliable research.

claims.text: The existing body of research on teaching methods is largely of poor quality and yields few useful results.
claims.source_passage: On teaching methods we already have a mountain of research, but those who dig there return empty-handed and discouraged. The mountain contains, to put the matter bluntly, mostly inferior ore.

claims.text: Composition research is frequently conducted by individuals lacking training in psychosocial experimental design and statistical inference.
claims.source_passage: The research is often conducted by persons untrained in the design and interpretation of psychosocial experiments; the variables are rarely kept under control; the statistical inferences are rarely sophisticated.

claims.text: Current judgments of writing quality in research rely on impressionism or mechanical counting rather than sound evaluation.
claims.source_passage: Worst of all, the judgment of writing quality is left either to impressionism or to a mechanical counting system.

claims.text: Effective research into teaching methods cannot be conducted until a reliable method for evaluating writing quality is established.
claims.source_passage: We cannot expect good research in teaching methods until we develop sound ways to evaluate writing quality.

claims.text: The criterion of relative readability should be adopted as the qualitative standard for research into teaching effectiveness.
claims.source_passage: I hope that in the future the criterion of relative readability will be accepted as the qualitative standard for well-grounded research into the effectiveness of teaching methods.

claims.text: The unique personalities of students and instructors are crucial factors that statistical research alone cannot fully account for.
claims.source_passage: The personality of the student and of the instructor will be crucial factors not covered in the statistics.

claims.text: Instructors should adapt their use of composition maxims to the specific needs of individual students.
claims.source_passage: The teacher will always need to emphasize different maxims with different students.

claims.text: Teachers often provide unhelpful feedback that lacks a clear pedagogical purpose.
claims.source_passage: The student usually has a clearer idea about his semantic intentions in his paper than the teacher has about his pedagogical purposes in making comments on it.

claims.text: Standardized maxims are needed for paper-marking to the same degree they are needed for paper-writing.
claims.source_passage: We need maxims for paper-marking fully as much as we need maxims for paper-writing.

claims.text: Written comments on student papers are likely the most effective device for teaching composition.
claims.source_passage: very probably written comments will turn out to be the most effective teaching device of all.

claims.text: Effective instruction in any practical art must build upon the practical knowledge the student already possesses.
claims.source_passage: To teach any practical art, a teacher always builds on the practical knowledge that his student already has.

claims.text: Learning occurs by integrating new information into an existing cognitive 'schema.'
claims.source_passage: The basis for this kind of instruction is the principle of the schema, to use Piaget’s term. A person learns something new by building on a schema already known.

claims.text: To teach any practical art, a teacher always builds on the practical knowledge that his student already has.
claims.source_passage: To teach any practical art, a teacher always builds on the practical knowledge that his student already has.

claims.text: A person learns something new by building on a schema already known.
claims.source_passage: A person learns something new by building on a schema already known, and in practical knowledge the already known form is a productive “schema” for performing a task.

claims.text: The history of prose followed a development of gradual changes in existing schemata, which individual students must to some extent repeat.
claims.source_passage: I have conjectured in Chapter 3 that the history of prose followed a similar development, one which individual students must to some extent repeat.

claims.text: The comment method has the highest importance in composition teaching because it is direct and widespread in all forms of practical instruction.
claims.source_passage: Because the comment method is so direct and so widespread in all forms of practical instruction, we can reasonably assume that it has the highest importance in composition teaching.

claims.text: Well-conceived commenting on papers is probably more effective than formal lectures on composition or Socratic questions about composition.
claims.source_passage: Well-conceived commenting on papers, then, is probably more effective than formal lectures on composition or even Socratic questions about composition.

claims.text: Pure lecturing is the least efficient method of teaching any practical art.
claims.source_passage: The most efficient method will probably turn out to be direct written commentary on the student’s performance, coupled with ad hoc discussions of the problems that have often cropped up in a particular group.

claims.text: The great advantages of the written commentary are its individuality and permanence.
claims.source_passage: The great advantages of the written commentary are its individuality and permanence.

claims.text: A teacher should comment on just two or three points in any paper to avoid overtaxing the student's capacities.
claims.source_passage: Comment on just two or three points in any paper. ... if the student is to keep the comments in mind when he writes again, they must be few in number, because his capacities are already heavily taxed by the act of writing.

claims.text: Correcting every fault in a student paper (the 'schoolmarm' method) is a pedagogical vice.
claims.source_passage: The worst vice of the schoolmarm is to correct everything.

claims.text: The chief talent of a writing teacher resides in the diagnostic ability to select the most important matters for comment for an individual student.
claims.source_passage: The chief talent of the teacher resides in the diagnostic ability that enables him to make this kind of selection.

claims.text: Commentary should be summarized in a usable form, preferably a short paragraph referring to specific examples.
claims.source_passage: Summarize the commentary in a usable form. ... The most useful form is probably a short paragraph referring to particular examples of a general point.

claims.text: The purpose of commentary is future, not past, performance, and it should not be treated as a defense of a grade.
claims.source_passage: Since the point of commentary is future, not past, performance, it should be put in a form that makes it available for future use by the student. Students and teachers will do well to conquer the assumption that the red ink on a paper is the defense of a grade.

claims.text: Encouraging comments generally produce better results than a scolding tone in composition instruction.
claims.source_passage: While a scolding tone may be just what is needed on occasion, the best results are likely to be produced by encouragement.

claims.text: A lot of rehearsal is needed to change a student’s productive schemata, making repetition of comments necessary.
claims.source_passage: A lot of rehearsal is needed to change a student’s productive schemata. ... Do not hesitate to repeat a comment over several papers.

claims.text: Teachers must make clear that comments deal with a craft to be learned, not with personal taste.
claims.source_passage: Make clear from the tone of the comments that they deal with a craft to be learned and not with the teacher’s personal taste.

claims.text: Repeating a specific comment over several different papers is an effective teaching device.
claims.source_passage: Do not hesitate to repeat a comment over several papers. If the teacher’s analysis is accurate, repetition of a comment is going to be an effective teaching device.

claims.text: Teachers should maintain abbreviated records of comments in their gradebooks to ensure continuity in a student's development.
claims.source_passage: Keep track of the comments, so that nothing of great importance for a particular student is omitted during the course. The teacher can accomplish this by keeping an abbreviated record in his gradebook.

claims.text: Writing instruction must be framed as the teaching of a craft rather than the expression of teacher preference.
claims.source_passage: Make clear from the tone of the comments that they deal with a craft to be learned and not with the teacher’s personal taste.

claims.text: Students generally believe they are writing to satisfy their teacher's personal whims.
claims.source_passage: Students of the subject usually believe that they are catering to their teacher’s whims.

claims.text: Students view teacher intervention in their prose style as an invasion of their privacy or personality.
claims.source_passage: Students, on their side, believe that tampering with their styles is tampering with their personalities. This makes the whole subject seem a serious invasion of privacy.

claims.text: Directed revision is the most important teaching device for writing after teacher commentary.
claims.source_passage: After comments, the most important device for teaching effective writing is probably directed revision.

claims.text: The primary goal of writing instruction is for students to internalize editorial principles.
claims.source_passage: Of course, the principles for directing a revision will reside ultimately within the student himself. To put them there is the goal of teaching—the goal of the teacher's comments.

claims.text: Learning to write is fundamentally identical to learning the principles of revision.
claims.source_passage: To learn writing is to learn principles of revision.

claims.text: Teaching methods are effective only to the extent that they teach students how to revise for readability.
claims.source_passage: It follows from this that teaching methods will be effective to the extent that they teach students how to revise their prose to make it readable.

claims.text: The energy expended on a first draft is less efficient for learning than the energy expended on applying revision principles.
claims.source_passage: The energy that a student expends in composing a first draft is diffused in several directions... Contrariwise, the energy he expends in applying revision principles is very directly aimed at learning those principles and improving his productive schemata as a writer.

claims.text: Publicly revising a text in class is an efficient method of instruction.
claims.source_passage: I also have found that to spend class time in publicly revising a text is an efficient teaching method.

claims.text: The 'workshop' model of composition increases student motivation and their sense of the subject's utility.
claims.source_passage: When the composition class is thus conceived as a workshop, the students perceive its utility to themselves and tend, in my experience, to be highly motivated.

claims.text: The readability of a text is functionally the same as its listenability.
claims.source_passage: This method has been validated by recent research which shows that the readability of a text is the same as its listenability,

claims.text: Oral comprehension tests accurately gauge the linearity of prose because the listener cannot reprocess previous sections.
claims.source_passage: The advantage of this oral method over visual presentation is that the listener is unable to circle back and reprocess an earlier section of the text. Hence, the linearity of the prose can be rather accurately gauged by such a comprehension test.

claims.text: Separating the role of teacher from the role of grader transforms the classroom dynamic into a collaborative partnership.
claims.source_passage: The pedagogical advantage of the method is the obvious one that teacher and student become colleagues in a joint enterprise, rather than suspicious adversaries.

claims.text: Objective progress in composition studies is dependent on the development of reliable tests of writing proficiency.
claims.source_passage: But unless reliable tests of writing proficiency are indeed developed, we cannot make serious progress in either teaching or research; nor can we make objective evaluations of either students or teachers of composition.

claims.text: A collaborative relationship where teacher and student are colleagues in a joint enterprise is superior to an adversarial relationship for teaching composition.
claims.source_passage: the obvious one that teacher and student become colleagues in a joint enterprise, rather than suspicious adversaries.

claims.text: The collaborative scheme of composition instruction can only work if reliable outside tests of writing proficiency are developed.
claims.source_passage: This scheme can work only if reliable outside tests are developed.

claims.text: Reliable tests of writing proficiency are a prerequisite for serious progress in teaching, research, and objective evaluation of students and teachers.
claims.source_passage: But unless reliable tests of writing proficiency are indeed developed, we cannot make serious progress in either teaching or research; nor can we make objective evaluations of either students or teachers of composition.

claims.text: The use of effective written commentary, revision-practice, and third-party evaluation makes the teaching of writing more efficient.
claims.source_passage: The use of these three techniques—effective written commentary, revision-practice in and out of class, and third-party evaluation of final results—will probably make the teaching of writing more efficient than it normally is.

claims.text: The optimal way to apply composition techniques across different age-levels is currently unknown and untested.
claims.source_passage: But the best way to apply these techniques is unknown, and their applicability to different age-levels is untested.

claims.text: The main craft of college-level composition is teaching students how to compose readable prose rather than just 'correct' or normalized prose.
claims.source_passage: my own practical experience has been gained in teaching and directing college-level courses, where the main craft to be taught is how to compose readable rather than “correct,” normalized prose.

claims.text: Prose readability depends on the normalization of spelling, grammar, punctuation, and word usage.
claims.source_passage: Yet readability does depend on the normalization of spelling, grammar, punctuation, and word usage.

claims.text: Spelling and punctuation are purely scribal conventions that require memorization and are best learned early.
claims.source_passage: Spelling and punctuation are purely scribal conventions, which need to be learned from the start by all native speakers. They are basic tools that require memorization, like the multiplication table and, like it, are best learned early.

claims.text: Grammar and word usage are not purely scribal because they belong to normalized oral speech as well as written speech.
claims.source_passage: But grammar and word usage are not purely scribal. They belong to normalized oral as well as written speech, and instruction in normalized speech is an odd kind of instruction to give native speakers of a language.

claims.text: Native speakers rarely make mistakes of grammar or word usage within their own native dialect.
claims.source_passage: A native speaker hardly ever makes a mistake of grammar or of word usage in his native dialect.

claims.text: Deviations from the grapholect are only true mistakes if they also deviate from the student's own native dialect.
claims.source_passage: Deviations from the normalized grapholect are true mistakes only when they are also deviations from the student’s own dialect, and these are almost always induced by the student’s attempt to use forms outside his ken.

claims.text: Readable writing is not identical to normalized, grapholectic writing.
claims.source_passage: it is not true that readable writing is the same as normalized, grapholectic writing.

claims.text: The composition teacher is only accidentally a teacher of linguistic correctness; their primary role is teaching literacy and readability.
claims.source_passage: The teacher of composition is only accidentally a teacher of correctness in grammar and usage.

claims.text: Teaching literacy involves the 'double burden' of teaching both genre conventions/readability and the conventions of a new dialect (the grapholect).
claims.source_passage: When this job is compounded by the additional need to teach the conventions of a new dialect, it is an immensely difficult undertaking. It is also a necessary one.

claims.text: There may be a single 'best way' or a small number of superior ways to organize a composition course.
claims.source_passage: There may in fact be a best way to organize a composition course or, at the least, a small number of ways that are better than any others.

claims.text: The trial-and-error approach to adapting composition courses to individual students and teachers has been unsuccessful.
claims.source_passage: The trial-and-error approach, which I formerly advocated on the ground that we teach best when we adapt to our students as well as to ourselves, has not brought us very far.

claims.text: It is currently unknown whether using a textbook is superior to not using one in composition instruction.
claims.source_passage: At present, we don’t even know whether using a textbook is better than not using one.

claims.text: The optimal way to organize a composition course or format a composition textbook for specific age groups is currently unknown.
claims.source_passage: Since we do not yet know the best way to organize a composition course for a particular age group, we cannot possibly know the best format for a composition textbook.

claims.text: Textbooks are not essential for competent instruction in freshman composition.
claims.source_passage: We already know that textbooks are not essential for competent instruction in freshman composition.

claims.text: There is no significant correlation between good composition teaching and the use or nonuse of textbooks.
claims.source_passage: They show no significant correlation between good composition teaching and the use or nonuse of textbooks.

claims.text: The student's actual learning in writing occurs through the individual process of producing-and-correcting rather than through uniform subject matter.
claims.source_passage: the student's actual learning takes place in the process of producing-and-correcting, which is an individual process for each student, rather than a uniform subject matter to be gleaned from a book.

claims.text: A high-quality manual of instruction likely speeds up the teaching and learning of a practical craft like writing.
claims.source_passage: On the other hand, it seems probable to me that a good manual of instruction will speed up the teaching and learning of a practical craft.

claims.text: Students apply corrections most effectively when they understand the underlying principles behind those corrections.
claims.source_passage: The student will be best able to apply the correction (that is, alter his productive schemata) if he understands the principles behind the correction.

claims.text: Existing composition textbooks lack genuine authority because they are not based on authoritative knowledge of the subject.
claims.source_passage: No doubt the gravest flaw in existing texts is their lack of genuine authority. They are not based upon authoritative knowledge of the subject of composition.

claims.text: Composition textbooks should not be thick books.
claims.source_passage: It is, I would say, an absolute fault in a composition textbook to be a thick book.

claims.text: Thick manuals intimidate students into believing that composition is a difficult craft and an arduous subject matter.
claims.source_passage: Those weighty volumes intimidate a student, making him assume that composition is both hard as a craft and also arduous to learn as a subject matter.

claims.text: Thick composition textbooks are padded with irrelevant exercises and examples.
claims.source_passage: In fact, the thick manuals are all padded. They preach brevity without practicing it, and they contain great amounts of irrelevant exercises and examples.

claims.text: Detailed source-books on grammar and usage are unnecessary for the craft of writing because they contain terms unknown to excellent writers.
claims.source_passage: Similarly, the thick source-books on grammar and usage are filled with terms unknown to many excellent writers, and are hence demonstrably unnecessary to the craft of writing.

claims.text: Native speakers do not require lengthy instruction in the refinements of grammar.
claims.source_passage: No native speaker needs to be taught lengthy refinements of grammar.

claims.text: The common writing errors (solecisms) committed by college students can be listed on a few pages.
claims.source_passage: The solecisms that most college students commit can be listed on a few pages.

claims.text: Using a thick composition book is pedagogically counterproductive and inefficient.
claims.source_passage: A thick composition book is pedagogically counterproductive. To use one in a composition course is analogous to lecturing on composition, and is just as inefficient.

claims.text: Teachers often select thick textbooks to provide an appearance of 'intellectual respectability' for courses that lack literary content.
claims.source_passage: The thickness of the manual then becomes the outward and visible sign of intellectual respectability in a course that eschews standard intellectual content.

claims.text: Large, 'elephantine' composition textbooks are inefficient for teaching the craft of writing.
claims.source_passage: Why do some teachers like to use these elephantine productions? ... When I have commented that the pedagogical disadvantages of a thick book seem to outweigh the hoped-for advantages, my colleagues at several institutions have usually conceded my point

claims.text: The selection of thick composition manuals is often motivated by a desire for 'intellectual respectability' rather than pedagogical effectiveness.
claims.source_passage: The thickness of the manual then becomes the outward and visible sign of intellectual respectability in a course that eschews standard intellectual content.

claims.text: Slender composition manuals provide significant pedagogical advantages by offering a center of authority and shared terminology.
claims.source_passage: it is probably a sound psychological principle to give composition intellectual respectability through the use of a good if slender manual. It serves to give the course an apparent center and an apparent authority which it might otherwise lack. It also gives common guidance to both teacher and students, through commonly shared principles and terminology.

claims.text: Manuals carry the danger of implying that writing is 'book-learning' rather than a set of 'habitual schemata for practicing a craft.'
claims.source_passage: The danger in using even a good manual is in the subtle implication that the learning to be gained is book-learning rather than habitual schemata for practicing a craft.

claims.text: Composition manuals are frequently flawed by a narrow and rigid focus on the specific genre of the college essay.
claims.source_passage: Another flaw in some of the manuals, thick and thin, is their narrow focus on the college essay. ... this narrowness of focus tempts handbook writers into rules and maxims that are too rigid even for college essays.

claims.text: Rigid structural formulas (like 'inverted funnel' conclusions) do not teach a student to write well.
claims.source_passage: No doubt a student who follows this formula will write better papers than one who has no schemata at all. But the formula will not teach him to write well.

claims.text: Students are better served by general and fundamental maxims of prose than by genre-specific conventions.
claims.source_passage: A student is better served, even in learning to write college essays, by being taught maxims that are more general and fundamental. This is so because the conventions of prose genres are tolerant and variable, whereas the principles of good prose consistently govern all genres, including the college essay.

claims.text: Extensive class time spent discussing the content of 'readers' is a dereliction of duty for a writing teacher.
claims.source_passage: To hold long class discussions about the content of these readers is in my view a dereliction of duty by the teacher. ... The time spent in a long discussion on some topical issue would be better spent in discussing how to revise a student’s paper, or by using class time for writing.

claims.text: Wide reading is a slow and inefficient method for learning the craft of writing.
claims.source_passage: It is true that wide reading has been a good way to learn about writing. But it has always been a slow and inefficient way to learn the craft, since we quickly forget the linguistic forms of the prose we read.

claims.text: Ideal writing textbooks should prioritize the 'production-correction' process and provide a limited number of persuasive, applicable maxims.
claims.source_passage: It should be a book that encourages the production-correction process between student and teacher. The maxims of correction (or revision) should be those used by the teacher and should reinforce his comments. ... They should be limited in number, and grouped according to their relative importance for their typical audience.

claims.text: The 'Elements of Style' by Strunk and White is an example of a textbook that successfully employs a short, manual-style format.
claims.source_passage: No existing text meets all these criteria, but one that meets several of them is Elements of Style by Strunk and White.

claims.text: Claims regarding desirable textbook qualities are 'informed guesses' in the absence of decisive experimental research.
claims.source_passage: Finally, I should stress that these inferences about desirable textbook qualities are merely informed guesses. Guesses are what we are stuck with in the absence of decisive experimental results.

claims.text: Research alone cannot solve educational problems stemming from cultural shifts like TV consumption or student motivation.
claims.source_passage: Research cannot change the television habits of the young, or encourage them to read more, or make them believe that good writing is important to learn. Research cannot transform all present teachers of writing into good writers.

claims.text: No existing writing textbook currently meets all the criteria for a theoretically sound instructional manual.
claims.source_passage: No existing text meets all these criteria, but one that meets several of them is Elements of Style by Strunk and White.

claims.text: Current recommendations for desirable textbook qualities are informed guesses rather than conclusions based on decisive experimental results.
claims.source_passage: Finally, I should stress that these inferences about desirable textbook qualities are merely informed guesses. Guesses are what we are stuck with in the absence of decisive experimental results.

claims.text: Composition research is incapable of altering the television habits, reading frequency, or motivational levels of young students.
claims.source_passage: Composition research cannot change the television habits of the young, or encourage them to read more, or make them believe that good writing is important to learn.

claims.text: The prevalence of television culture makes intensive research into composition efficiency more important rather than less.
claims.source_passage: The best answer to these truths is that TV culture makes intensive research into composition all the more important.

claims.text: A primary objective of composition research is to maximize the efficiency of teaching and learning to make better use of limited time.
claims.source_passage: After all, one object of such research is to make composition teaching and learning more efficient, and thereby to make better use of the limited time that is now available for reading and writing.

claims.text: Inefficient methods of teaching composition were viable in the past only because they did not have to compete with modern distractions like television.
claims.source_passage: Inefficient methods, like spending one’s days and nights in the study of Addison, could work when Addison did not compete with Archie Bunker.

claims.text: Public concern over the decline in writing skills is a necessary precursor to improving the quality of composition research.
claims.source_passage: The most hopeful augury is the present public outcry over the decline in writing skills. Public concern means increased support for an effort to reverse the decline.

claims.text: Professional talent is historically drawn to fields that offer high professional status.
claims.source_passage: This rise in the status of the subject will then help to raise the quality of composition research, because professional talent has always pursued professional status.

claims.text: The status of composition studies within universities has historically been at the absolute bottom of the academic hierarchy.
claims.source_passage: And in the past, the professional status of composition at the universities has been at the bottom of the heap.

claims.text: Without proper coordination, an influx of research interest in composition will lead to wasteful duplication and a 'gold rush' mentality.
claims.source_passage: The danger in the new situation is that composition research will become as chaotic as the California gold rush. Good work will be as hard to find as nuggets in a well-panned stream. Duplication of effort will abound.

claims.text: The composition field requires machinery to keep researchers informed of ongoing work to avoid wasteful effort.
claims.source_passage: But in order to avoid wasteful duplication of effort, I do suggest that we create machinery for keeping researchers informed about the work-in-progress of others.

claims.text: The lack of refereed journals and meticulous outside scrutiny has historically degraded the scientific standards of composition research.
claims.source_passage: In the past, we have had no reliable system of quality control, no refereed journals of recognized authority.

claims.text: There is a fundamental distinction between an academic 'subject matter' (like psychology) and a social 'mission' (like composition).
claims.source_passage: The difference between a field like composition research and a field like experimental psychology is the difference between a mission and a subject matter.

claims.text: Academic fields often neglect socially valuable subproblems because solving them does not confer prestige or professional advancement within that discipline.
claims.source_passage: These subproblems, essential to the mission, are neglected in the fields themselves, because they are not very interesting problems within those fields. ... the subproblem does not attract an effort within the universities proportional to its social value.

claims.text: There is currently no dependable scientific answer regarding the optimal frequency, length, or sequence of writing assignments for college freshmen.
claims.source_passage: What pattern of writing assignments will tend to promote the fastest improvements in the writing ability of college freshmen? This is a question that every composition teacher asks himself and his colleagues—a crucial question which has received no dependable answer.

claims.text: Composition teachers currently lack dependable answers to fundamental pedagogical questions regarding assignment frequency, length, and topic choice.
claims.source_passage: Is it best to assign two short papers every week? One short paper a week? To vary the length and frequency of the assignments? To stress intellectual topics? To stress personal topics? ... a crucial question which has received no dependable answer.

claims.text: Reliable answers to composition pedagogy questions can only be generated under the guidance of first-class experimental psychologists.
claims.source_passage: Reliable answers would be forthcoming only if the necessary experiments were conducted under the guidance of a first-class experimental psychologist, one familiar with the design of experiments and with such lurking dangers as premature conclusions, uncontrolled variables, and insufficient data bases.

claims.text: High-level experimental psychologists find composition research uninteresting because it has few basic implications for the field of psychology and does not enhance their professional reputation.
claims.source_passage: And he would know that, when the final results were in, they would do nothing to enhance his reputation as an experimental psychologist. They would probably have few basic implications in psychology. ... Hence, to a first-class experimental psychologist, the whole problem will seem trivial and uninteresting, not to say tedious, time-consuming, and messy.

claims.text: Effective composition research requires a multidisciplinary approach involving experts in sociology, educational psychology, and administrative cooperation.
claims.source_passage: Some of the crucial variables might turn out to be social in character rather than psychological, and since the best pattern of assignments might be different for different social groups, the experimenter would need the help of an expert in sociology. He would also need the assistance of an expert in educational psychology, and the cooperation of teachers, department chairmen, deans, and other college administrators.

claims.text: The deficiency in practical composition research should be addressed by instituting mission-oriented research enterprises.
claims.source_passage: Weinberg suggests that this kind of deficiency in practical research can be made good by instituting mission-oriented research enterprises.

claims.text: Mission-oriented research in composition must be integrated into the university structure rather than remaining extramural.
claims.source_passage: Somehow, mission-oriented research in composition will need to be integrated into the universities themselves. And the universities will need to develop appropriate arrangements and incentives for conducting it.

claims.text: National teaching organizations like the NCTE, CCCC, and MLA are not equipped to evaluate or lead scientific research projects.
claims.source_passage: The NCTE, the CCCC, and the MLA are organizations of teachers, not scientific researchers. They are not equipped to pass judgment on the quality of scientific research. They have no special competence in the organization and evaluation of large, multidiscipline scientific missions.

claims.text: The proper role of teaching organizations is to identify the goals of research missions rather than providing scientific guidance.
claims.source_passage: The role of the teaching organizations is to help identify the missions themselves—the goals to be achieved.

claims.text: Universities should seek scientific guidance for composition research from institutions like the National Science Foundation.
claims.source_passage: For assistance, the universities will need to turn to institutions like the National Science Foundation, and to authoritative persons with experience in the organization of mission-oriented research.

claims.text: The development of reliable methods for evaluating writing quality is the essential prerequisite for all future research in the field.
claims.source_passage: One goal at the top of everyone’s list will be to develop reliable methods of evaluating the quality of writing. This is the sine qua non of all future composition research, since without it we have no way of testing anything else.

claims.text: Pedagogical progress in writing cannot be identified or verified without objective evaluation standards.
claims.source_passage: We cannot find out for instance which assignment patterns yield the best results unless we can identify what the best results are. We can’t find out which maxims encourage greatest progress unless we can identify progress.

claims.text: The problems of composition research are not amenable to solution by individual humanistic research alone.
claims.source_passage: The problems are not amenable to solution by individual humanistic research, although we humanists have been called upon to solve them.

claims.text: The professional status of composition teaching is currently increasing within the academic hierarchy.
claims.source_passage: The professional status of composition teaching is rising. If this revival of interest in the subject is coupled with practical knowledge based on sound research, [then success is possible].

claims.text: The academic job market currently favors PhDs with credentials in composition over those in literary history.
claims.source_passage: Ph.D’s with credentials in composition are getting teaching jobs, while Ph.D’s in literary history are driving taxis.

claims.text: Effective composition research requires the cooperation of specialists from education, psychology, and the social sciences.
claims.source_passage: We need the help of our colleagues in education, psychology, and the social sciences.

claims.text: Advancing composition research requires the expertise of organizers experienced in mission-oriented research.
claims.source_passage: We also need help from experienced organizers of mission-oriented research.

claims.text: There is currently a renewed interest in the subject of composition within the academic community.
claims.source_passage: The most promising sign that this large-scale effort will be undertaken is the renewed interest in the subject of composition.

claims.text: The general quality of writing will improve only if the current interest in composition is coupled with practical knowledge derived from sound research.
claims.source_passage: If this revival of interest in the subject is coupled with practical knowledge based on sound research, the general quality of writing is likely to improve.

claims.text: Literacy is universally accepted as a primary goal of schooling by all educational planners.
claims.source_passage: Everyone accepts literacy as a goal of schooling, but the planners of school curricula are not always sure just where the skill of writing should be taught.

claims.text: Literature and logic do not need to be presented in large doses to a captive audience in a required writing course.
claims.source_passage: But these subjects are offered in the universities; they do not need to be presented in large doses to a captive audience in a required course whose announced goal is the teaching of writing.

claims.text: There exists an inextinguishable belief among educators that 'clear' writing and 'clear' thinking are direct reflections of each other.
claims.source_passage: ...a concession to the inextinguishable belief that “clear” writing and “clear” thinking reflect each other.

claims.text: In written discourse, the text itself serves as the primary context of the utterance, unlike oral speech which relies on external situations.
claims.source_passage: In written discourse, you have to choose a design and stick to it, because the discourse itself is the chief context of the utterance.

claims.text: The only legitimate defense for boring writing is that the author specifically intended the reader to be bored.
claims.source_passage: Being boring is hard to defend on any grounds. I can think of only one defense of it—that the writer wants to be boring.

claims.text: The paragraph is to a text what a clause is to a sentence and what a phrase is to a clause.
claims.source_passage: It is to a text as a clause is to a sentence and a phrase is to a clause.

claims.text: The paradoxical effect of using integrative repetitions is that it forces the writer to vary their syntax and sentence length to avoid monotony.
claims.source_passage: For, paradoxically, the use of integrative repetitions in nearby phrases will force variations on the writer by compelling him to rearrange his word sequences.

claims.text: Teachers and students should abandon the assumption that 'red ink' comments on a paper are merely a defense of the assigned grade.
claims.source_passage: Students and teachers will do well to conquer the assumption that the red ink on a paper is the defense of a grade.

claims.text: Any writer’s first draft is effectively the product of an internal revision process where certain forms have already been rejected.
claims.source_passage: Any writer’s first draft is already the product of a revision process in which some forms have been rejected in favor of others on the basis of editorial principles which the writer has internalized.

claims.text: The historically low quality of composition research is due to researchers being untrained in the design and interpretation of psychosocial experiments.
claims.source_passage: The research is often conducted by persons untrained in the design and interpretation of psychosocial experiments; the variables are rarely kept under control; the statistical inferences are rarely sophisticated.

evidence.description: The observation that many graduate students in literature possess deep subject knowledge yet lack the ability to write well.
L. T. Milic's 1965 argument regarding the conflict between the assumptions of literary study and composition teaching.
evidence.source_reference: Milic, L. T. 'Theories of Style and Their Implications for the Teaching of Composition,' College Composition and Communication 16 (1965): 2-6.

evidence.description: The persistence of 'logical fallacies' chapters in composition handbooks despite the decline of formal logic as a core subject.
Huizinga's historical account of Renaissance humanism's priorities.
The observation that successful writing teachers are moving away from teaching formal linguistic terminology.
Legal statutes and technical manuals are examples of important writing that is judged by quality but is not persuasive or emotive in intent.
The analogy to teaching drawing, where a student produces work and receives feedback from a master, rather than studying art history.
The usage of the term 'bonae litterae' by Erasmus during the Renaissance to signify what moderns call composition.
Spencer's own admission that 'magnificent' may be better than 'grand' and complex sentences better than short ones depending on semantic intention.
A list of 11 prescriptive maxims from Strunk and White emphasizing clarity and brevity.
evidence.source_reference: W. Strunk and E. B. White, The Elements of Style (New York, 1959)

evidence.description: Ten maxims from Ernest Gowers focusing on the 'Plain Words' approach to writing.
evidence.source_reference: E. Gowers, The Complete Plain Words (London, 1954)

evidence.description: Maxims from McCrimmon emphasizing purpose, outlining, and paragraph unity.
evidence.source_reference: J. M. McCrimmon, Writing with a Purpose (Boston, 1975)

evidence.description: Maxims from Crews focusing on tone, vivid language, and transitions.
evidence.source_reference: F. Crews, The Random House Handbook (New York, 1974)

evidence.description: Comparison of stylistic rules from five major handbooks (Strunk, Gowers, McCrimmon, Crews, and Lucas), showing high overlap in advice regarding concrete language, brevity, and paragraph unity.
The specific case of Sir Ernest Gowers's handbook, which omitted basic mechanics to focus on 'humanity' and 'simplicity' because it was originally a government white paper for civil servants.
Experimental study establishing that positive statements are processed more quickly than negative ones because they are more 'linear'.
evidence.source_reference: T. Trabasso, H. Rollins, and E. Shaughnessy, 'Storage and Verification in Processing Concepts,' Cognitive Psychology 2 (1971): 239-89.

evidence.description: Experiments cited demonstrating that meaning is extracted more reliably from concrete language than from abstract language.
evidence.source_reference: Not explicitly named in text; referred to as 'recent experiments'.

evidence.description: Linguistic comparison showing 'the book is shut' is easier to process than 'the book is not open'.
Stylistic comparison showing 'The pen is mightier than the sword' is more effective than 'Writing is more effective than warfare'.
A study on comprehension factors regarding memory for abstract versus concrete sentences.
evidence.source_reference: M. K. Johnson, J. D. Bransford, S. E. Nyberg, J. J. Cleary, “Comprehension Factors in Interpreting Memory for Abstract and Concrete Sentences,” Journal of Verbal Learning and Verbal Behavior 8 (1972): 451-54.

evidence.description: The observation that transcribed oral discourse is often unintelligible due to false starts and shifts in subject.
evidence.source_passage: Recorded oral discourse is punctuated with false starts, changes of mind, changes of subject in the middle of sentences. When transcribed, such discourse approaches unintelligibility.

evidence.description: The psychological principle of short-term expectancy, previously discussed on pages 102-103.
Psychological findings regarding the rapid decay of linguistic/lexical memory in human subjects.
A comparative revision of a dense sentence regarding 'trigram frequency' to demonstrate that clarity comes from word grouping rather than just sentence length.
evidence.source_passage: Once again the data force the conclusion that trigram frequency, as measured by counting the frequency of three-letter sequences in printed text, does not predict learning when those trigrams are responses in paired-associate lists.

evidence.description: Three sentences by Edward Sapir illustrating how the requirement for adjacent repetition (repeating 'changes' and 'borrowing') induces a variation in syntax (an 'of' clause).
A student essay entitled 'My Opinion of the Anthony Burgess Essay,' which demonstrates common structural and stylistic flaws, and the ineffective teacher comments that followed it.
