The pedagogical methods used by tennis coaches, such as relating the racket grip to 'shaking hands,' to illustrate the principle of teaching through existing schemata.
Gombrich's observation that illusionist painters evolved their techniques by making gradual changes to existing schemata for representing the world.
The analogy of a tennis coach teaching a beginner to grip a racket like 'shaking hands' to demonstrate building on existing knowledge.
A specific diagnostic example using a previous passage on 'Burgess' where the main fault was a lack of a cohesive theme to integrate sentences.
E.B. White's characterization of his teacher, Strunk, which suggests that students often perceive clear instruction as the expression of a teacher's personality/whims.
Research showing that the readability of a text correlates strongly with how easily it is understood when heard.
evidence.source_reference: I. E. Fang, 'The Easy Listening Formula,' Journal of Broadcasting 11 (1966)
evidence.source_passage: This method has been validated by recent research which shows that the readability of a text is the same as its listenability, 11

evidence.description: The author's personal classroom experience regarding the frequency of revision assignments.
evidence.source_passage: My own experience as a composition teacher leads me to believe that it is a good technique to make about every third assignment a revision assignment based on the teacher’s earlier comments.

evidence.description: The author's use of oral comprehension tests in class to demonstrate prose linearity.
evidence.source_passage: I have read aloud from student papers and then tested the group’s understanding of what was read.

evidence.description: The author's own professional experience directing and teaching college-level composition courses.
Frederick Crews' assertion in the Random House Handbook that there is no one 'right' way to organize a composition course.
Course evaluations from a freshman composition course show consistent instructor performance patterns but no significant correlation between textbook use and teaching quality.
Observation that many excellent writers are unfamiliar with the technical terms found in thick grammar and usage source-books.
The author's anecdotal survey of colleagues at several institutions regarding textbook choice.
Standard rules in popular manuals, such as placing a thesis at the end of the first paragraph.
Reference to the enduring utility and style of Strunk and White's 'Elements of Style'.
The study of Joseph Addison is cited as an example of a time-intensive, inefficient method that was only viable in a pre-television era.
Archie Bunker is used as a cultural symbol for the modern distractions that compete with academic reading and writing tasks.
Weinberg's theory regarding the distinction between 'missions' and 'subject matters' and the tendency of disciplines to neglect mission-essential subproblems.
evidence.source_reference: A. Weinberg, Reflections on Big Science (Cambridge, Mass., 1967)

evidence.description: The Modern Language Association (MLA) recently established a specific division for composition studies.
Anecdotal observation of Ph.D. employment patterns where composition specialists are hired over literary historians.
Professors from other prestigious fields like comparative literature are beginning to write on composition.
Anecdotal evidence of the job market where literary history PhDs are underemployed (driving taxis) compared to composition PhDs.

concepts.author_definition: Drawing correct inferences from given premises; logical validity.
concepts.common_alternative: Often conflated with the ability to write clearly.
concepts.source_passage: Clear thinking means drawing correct inferences from the given premises.

concepts.author_definition: An unambiguous and readable expression of meaning; relative readability.
concepts.common_alternative: Often conflated with the ability to think logically.
concepts.source_passage: Clear writing means an unambiguous and readable expression of one’s meaning.

concepts.author_definition: The position that composition is an autonomous craft that should be taught independently of literature, logic, or other subject matters.
concepts.source_passage: I must therefore assume that some of my readers will also be troubled by my separatist views.

concepts.author_definition: The literary assumption that style and meaning are inseparable and must be studied as a single unit.
concepts.source_passage: The study of style in literature is a study of the fusion of form with content.

concepts.author_definition: A subject taught through the cycle of student production and expert commentary, focused on gaining functional skill rather than theoretical subject-matter knowledge.
The pedagogical position that instruction in writing can and should be separated from instruction in formal logic or specific subject-matter content.
Literally 'good letters'; used by Hirsch to historicaly ground composition as a core cultural and educational pursuit akin to the Renaissance humanists' focus.
Herbert Spencer's idea that the best style is that which conveys the most meaning with the least reader effort; Hirsch accepts its validity but critques its lack of practical specificity.
Specific, situational imperatives found in composition handbooks meant to guide student writing decisions, which are useful but not universally applicable.
concepts.common_alternative: Universal rules of grammar or style.
concepts.source_passage: But “practical” maxims have their own problems. If they are many they can confuse the student, and if they are few they can mislead him. Probably no single maxim of composition holds for all cases.

concepts.author_definition: A theoretical law that remains true across all cases, such as the principle of psychological economy, providing a more reliable foundation for judgment than situational maxims.
concepts.common_alternative: Theoretical abstractions with no practical use in the classroom.
concepts.source_passage: Because of this exigency, a general principle (being always true) is often more practical than a directly practical maxim.

concepts.author_definition: The foundational linguistic and psychological reasons that explain why certain maxims work; the 'why' behind the 'how' of stylistic rules.
A transferable ability to write well across various genres, predicated on the mastery of general linguistic and psychological principles rather than genre-specific rules.
Directly usable, concrete rules of thumb found in handbooks (e.g., 'Use the active voice') that generally improve readability but are subject to exceptions.
The property of a statement where its linguistic structure corresponds directly to the cognitive process of understanding, making it faster to process (e.g., positive statements vs. negatives).
The point at which a reader can complete the meaning of a clause or phrase, achieved more rapidly when related words are kept together.
Stylistic maxims that are generally sound but contain implicit exceptions based on the writer's specific semantic purpose.
The psychological tendency to expect a subsequent event or linguistic structure to resemble a preceding one when they share common features.
The initial, tentative interpretation a reader makes about the intended meaning of a clause or sentence while reading.
Integrative linguistic devices (like 'however' or 'moreover') that signal the logical relationship between a current clause and preceding ones.
The repetition of a word or phrase from a preceding clause in the following one to maintain semantic continuity.
Integrative writing techniques that operate between the level of the individual clause and large-scale thematic organization.
The principle that effective writing minimizes the mental energy or 'friction' required by the reader to extract the intended meaning.
The repetition of forms, words, or meanings in nearby phrases or clauses to fulfill expectations and integrate the sequence.
Words like 'however' that predict a coming qualification, forcing the reader to remember and thus implicitly repeat the adjacent meaning being qualified.
The repetition of thematic elements between neighboring clauses, achieved via word duplication, pronouns, or synonyms.
A qualitative standard for evaluating writing quality in pedagogical research, moving away from subjective impressionism or mechanical counting.
A systematic set of principles to guide teachers in providing effective, purposeful feedback on student compositions.
An existing, productive form of practical knowledge in the learner's mind upon which new learning is built.
concepts.common_alternative: Piaget's term for a cognitive structure or mental representation.

concepts.author_definition: The direct, individualized written feedback on a student's performance, viewed as the most efficient method for teaching the craft of writing.
The specific internal cognitive structures a student uses to produce prose, which require rehearsal and repetitive correction to change.
A classroom structure where time is dedicated to individual writing and revising rather than lecture, fostering a sense of utility and collaboration.
The degree to which a spoken text can be understood by a listener on first hearing, used as a proxy for the readability and linearity of prose.
Purely written conventions like spelling and punctuation that have no oral equivalent and must be learned through rote memorization.

concepts.term: Double burden of the literacy teacher
concepts.author_definition: The dual challenge of teaching the structural conventions of a standard dialect (grapholect) while simultaneously teaching the rhetorical principles of readability and genre.
Language that follows the standard conventions of the grapholect, existing in both oral and written forms, as opposed to local or native dialects.
The individual, iterative process where a student writes and receives feedback, which the author identifies as the primary site of actual learning.
The underlying rationale or rule that explains a specific correction; understanding this principle allows students to alter their 'productive schemata.'
An outward appearance of academic rigor, often sought by teachers through the use of thick manuals when a course lacks traditional subject matter like literature.
Mental structures or patterns for practicing writing as a craft rather than as rote 'book-learning.'
The essential pedagogical cycle where a student produces prose and receives corrective feedback (revision maxims) from a teacher.
The ability of a pedagogical method to produce the greatest improvement in writing skills within the increasingly limited time available for instruction in a media-saturated culture.

A 'mission' is a field defined by a social goal (e.g., teaching writing) requiring subproblems from many fields; a 'subject matter' is a traditional academic discipline (e.g., psychology) defined by its own internal theoretical interests.
The application of high scientific standards, peer review, and refereed journals to research findings before they are accepted by the field.
Large-scale, multidisciplinary team projects that cut across standard academic disciplines to solve specific practical problems, typically rewarded differently than standard university research.
concepts.common_alternative: Basic research or disciplinary-focused research driven by individual scholars.
concepts.source_passage: Weinberg suggests that this kind of deficiency in practical research can be made good by instituting mission-oriented research enterprises. Such team projects already exist outside the universities... These extramural team efforts cut across the standard disciplines

concepts.author_definition: The 'sine qua non' or essential prerequisite of composition research; a reliable method of identifying 'best results' or 'progress' that allows for the testing of pedagogical maxims.
The academic standing and legitimacy of composition as a specialized field of study and a viable career path for scholars.
The projection of specialized or mysterious authority by a writer (especially a bureaucrat) over the reader.
The stylistic goal of projecting a shared, accessible human presence in writing rather than pomposity.
The assumption that instructor feedback (red ink) exists solely to justify a low mark rather than to teach the craft.
The author's metaphor for the existing mountain of poor-quality composition research.
The standardized version of a language used in writing, distinct from oral dialects.

cases.name: The University Composition Course
cases.description: The author discusses the debate within university contexts about whether to combine writing instruction with subject matter like literature or to teach it as a separate skill. He notes that literature graduate students often fail to write well despite their expertise, serving as a negative example for the 'fusion' theory.
key_passages: I am most familiar with it in the university context, where the lines are usually drawn between those who favor combining composition with some university subject like literature and those who regard all extrinsic subject matter... as irrelevant
Numbers of graduate students in literature are unable to write well, yet they do demonstrably know a great deal about literature

cases.name: The instruction of drawing
cases.description: Hirsch uses the pedagogical model of a drawing class—where students draw and the teacher critiques the output—as the ideal template for composition instruction, contrasting it with lecturing on theory.
key_passages: To learn how to write is to gain practical, not theoretical, knowledge. Writing needs to be taught as a practical, not a theoretical, subject. The teacher of drawing asks his students to make drawings, and then comments on what his students have produced.

cases.name: Composition Handbooks Analysis
cases.description: Hirsch excerpts and compares maxims from five representative handbooks (Strunk, Gowers, McCrimmon, Crews, and Lucas) to show the 'accumulated practical wisdom' of the field and its relationship to psychological principles.
key_passages: To do this, I shall excerpt some typical imperatives from five representative handbooks: Strunk, Gowers, McCrimmon, Crews, and Lucas.
I believe it will be illuminating to analyze some of the accumulated practical wisdom of the handbooks in the light of the preceding chapters of this book.

cases.name: Sir Ernest Gowers and the British Civil Service
cases.description: Gowers's handbook was originally a government white paper designed to reform the pompous and confusing style of official bureaucratic reports and letters. Because his audience was already literate in basic grammar, Gowers focused on clarity and brevity, illustrating that the 'importance' of specific rules is contingent on the audience's existing skills.
key_passages: Gowers’s excellent handbook, though widely used in Britain by teachers and journalists, was originally addressed to government bureaucrats.
Since his book was aimed at the characteristic failings of bureaucratic writing, he chose not to discuss coordination, transitions, or even sentence variety.

cases.name: Comparison of Writing Handbooks
cases.description: The author compares five writing handbooks (McCrimmon, Crews, Lucas, Strunk, Gowers) to show that while they provide similar maxims, they differ in which they emphasize (e.g., Strunk alone emphasizes positive statements).
key_passages: Among the five handbooks from which I have extracted writing maxims, only the one by Strunk gives prominence to the rule 'Put statements in positive form.'
The stylistic rules just mentioned are all concerned with making clauses easier to process.

cases.name: Transcribed Oral Discourse
cases.description: The author uses the example of verbatim transcripts of speech to demonstrate that oral language relies on external context and intonation. Without these, the 'false starts' and 'changes of mind' inherent in speech make the text unintelligible, proving that written discourse requires a stable internal design.
key_passages: Recorded oral discourse is punctuated with false starts, changes of mind, changes of subject in the middle of sentences. When transcribed, such discourse approaches unintelligibility.

cases.name: Trigram Frequency Revision
cases.description: The author takes a complex sentence about psychological research on trigrams and revises it into three sentences. He argues the resulting improvement in readability is not due to length but to keeping related words together and omitting needless qualifications between nouns and verbs.
key_passages: Once again the data force the conclusion that trigram frequency, as measured by counting the frequency of three-letter sequences in printed text, does not predict learning when those trigrams are responses in paired-associate lists.
My main reasons for breaking up the long sentence were to keep related words together and to omit needless intervening words.

cases.name: Edward Sapir's Prose Style
cases.description: The author analyzes a passage by Sapir where the themes of 'changes' and 'borrowing' are repeated across sentences. Hirsch notes that to achieve this integrative repetition, Sapir began a new paragraph with an 'of' clause that deviates from ordinary syntax, proving that repetition leads to syntactic variation.
key_passages: The following three sentences by Sapir illustrate how the need for adjacent repetitions will induce variations in syntax and sentence length

The main pattern of repetition here is changes—changes and borrowing—borrowing. In order to achieve this integrative repetition, Sapir began his new paragraph with an of clause that varys from ordinary syntax.

cases.name: The Burgess Essay Student Critique
cases.description: A case of a typical 'C-level' composition student critiquing Anthony Burgess. The student displays some insight but lacks control over sentence structure (separating subject and predicate). The teacher's feedback is criticized for focusing on minor grammatical points ('is referring to' vs 'refers to') rather than helping the student improve their future writing strategy.
key_passages: The comments which the teacher did in fact write down on this passage were not very helpful.
A C-level composition student is often doing a better job than his teacher in performing his appointed task.

cases.name: The 'Schoolmarm' Method
cases.description: A common pedagogical failure where a teacher attempts to correct every single error on a student's paper, leading to cognitive overload and failure to learn.
key_passages: The worst vice of the schoolmarm is to correct everything.

cases.name: E.B. White and William Strunk
cases.description: E.B. White's perception of his teacher Strunk's instructions. White viewed Strunk's demand for positive phrasing as an expression of Strunk's 'forthright personality' rather than a universal principle of effective communication.
key_passages: E. B. White thought and apparently still thinks that his teacher, Strunk, was expressing his own definite and forthright personality when he ordered his students to put statements in a positive form.
contestable_aspects: The degree to which Strunk's style was actually idiosyncratic vs. principled.
White's subjective interpretation of his mentor.

cases.name: The University of Virginia Composition Program
cases.description: The author references the composition course he directs, using its internal course evaluations to support the claim that textbooks do not significantly impact the quality of instruction.
key_passages: This inference is based on course evaluations that have been in use for several years in the composition course that I direct.
They show no significant correlation between good composition teaching and the use or nonuse of textbooks.

thinkers.name: L. T. Milic
thinkers.key_idea: The fundamental conflict between the assumptions of literary study (form/content fusion) and composition (form/content separation).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Milic's 1965 argument as a 'cogent' and 'never refuted' description of why writing instruction must be separated from literary study.
key_passages: This conflict of assumptions between literary study and composition teaching was accurately described by Milic some years ago, and his cogent argument has never been refuted.

thinkers.name: J. Huizinga
thinkers.key_idea: Renaissance humanists viewed prose style as a central cultural objective.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Huizinga's historical observation to justify the high status of composition as a 'humanistic' goal despite its separation from literature.
key_passages: Yet the humanists of the Renaissance thought that a good prose style was a chief goal of culture, and a sound case can be made for their view.

thinkers.name: Erasmus
thinkers.key_idea: The revival of culture (Renaissance) was a revival of 'bonae litterae' (humane letters).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch equates Erasmus's focus on 'bonae litterae' with the modern subject of composition to elevate the latter's importance in the university curriculum.
key_passages: When Erasmus spoke of the great cultural renaissance of his time as a revival of bonae litterae, he meant a revival of what we would now call composition.

thinkers.name: Herbert Spencer
thinkers.key_idea: All stylistic maxims can be reduced to the single principle of economy.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch admits Spencer was theoretically successful in reducing maxims to a single principle, but argues the principle is too abstract to be useful to students without more concrete guidance.
key_passages: Herbert Spencer’s attempt to reduce all stylistic maxims to a single principle was in fact a successful attempt. But the practical difficulty with Spencer’s principle of economy is its generality.

thinkers.name: William Strunk
thinkers.key_idea: Prescriptive, imperative-based rules for style (e.g., 'Omit needless words').
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Strunk's imperative-based format as a model for analyzing other handbooks and acknowledges his rules as 'tried and true' applications of deeper principles.
key_passages: Following the patterns set by Strunk, I shall phrase all the maxims as imperatives
the tried and true maxims of composition turn out to correlate very well with the psychological principles of readability.

thinkers.name: Sir Ernest Gowers
thinkers.key_idea: Focused on 'Plain Words,' simplicity, and humanity in official writing.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch treats Gowers as an example of an 'excellent' handbook that correctly tailored its advice to a specific audience, though this tailoring makes it an outlier when compared to freshman composition texts.
key_passages: Gowers’s excellent handbook... was originally addressed to government bureaucrats.
He wanted official letters to be less pompous and confusing, to say what they had to say, and to project a common humanity

thinkers.name: Johnson, Bransford, Nyberg, and Cleary
thinkers.key_idea: Factors affecting memory and comprehension of abstract vs concrete sentences.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites their 1972 study to provide psychological grounding for the stylistic preference for concrete over abstract words.
key_passages: M. K. Johnson, J. D. Bransford, S. E. Nyberg, J. J. Cleary, “Comprehension Factors in Interpreting Memory for Abstract and Concrete Sentences”

thinkers.name: Edward Sapir
thinkers.key_idea: Used specific patterns of thematic repetition and syntactic variation in his scholarly prose.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Sapir's prose as an exemplar of the psycholinguistic principles of readability he is advocating, specifically the 'paradoxical' effect of repetition forcing variation.
key_passages: The following three sentences by Sapir illustrate how the need for adjacent repetitions will induce variations in syntax and sentence length

thinkers.name: Jean Piaget
thinkers.key_idea: The principle of the 'schema' as the basis for learning.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch applies Piaget's psychological concept of the schema to the practical art of teaching composition.
key_passages: The basis for this kind of instruction is the principle of the schema, to use Piaget’s term.

thinkers.name: E.H. Gombrich
thinkers.key_idea: The development of illusionist painting based on gradual changes in existing schemata.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Gombrich's history of art as a parallel for how practical knowledge and skills are acquired and taught.
key_passages: Gombrich has suggested that illusionist painters followed a development based on gradual changes in the existing schemata for representing the world.

thinkers.name: E. B. White
thinkers.key_idea: Believed his teacher Strunk's commands were expressions of Strunk's 'forthright personality' rather than objective craft rules.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses White's view as an example of a common student misconception that writing standards are based on teacher whim or 'personal taste.'
key_passages: E. B. White thought and apparently still thinks that his teacher, Strunk, was expressing his own definite and forthright personality

thinkers.name: Frederick Crews
thinkers.key_idea: That there is no single 'right' way to organize a composition course; courses should be flexible.
thinkers.source_work: Instruction Manual for The Random House Handbook (1974)
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch argues that Crews may have overstated the need for flexibility and that a scientifically 'best' way to organize instruction likely exists, noting that the trial-and-error approach has failed.
key_passages: I think Crews may have overstated the same point when he flatly said “that there isn’t any one ‘right’ way to organize” a composition course.

thinkers.name: Strunk and White
thinkers.key_idea: The use of a short, concise manual ('Elements of Style') to teach writing.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites them as the closest existing model to his ideal textbook because of their brevity and focus.
key_passages: No existing text meets all these criteria, but one that meets several of them is Elements of Style by Strunk and White.

thinkers.name: Alvin Weinberg
thinkers.key_idea: The distinction between 'Big Science' missions and traditional academic subject matters, where missions are often neglected by the very disciplines required to solve their subproblems.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch applies Weinberg's sociological analysis of science to the field of composition, arguing that composition's 'hybrid' nature is typical of mission-oriented research.
key_passages: The difference between a field like composition research and a field like experimental psychology is the difference between a mission and a subject matter. The implications of this distinction have been very ably drawn by Weinberg in his book Reflections on Big Science.

thinkers.name: Joseph Addison
thinkers.key_idea: An 18th-century prose stylist whose works were historically used as models for imitation in composition classes.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Addison as an example of a pedagogical method that was 'inefficient' and only sustainable when students lacked modern distractions.
key_passages: Inefficient methods, like spending one’s days and nights in the study of Addison, could work when Addison did not compete with Archie Bunker.

thinkers.name: A. Weinberg
thinkers.key_idea: The concept of 'mission-oriented research' as a way to address practical research deficiencies via team projects.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch adopts Weinberg's concept to explain why composition research has stalled and how it might be reorganized through multidisciplinary teams.
key_passages: Weinberg suggests that this kind of deficiency in practical research can be made good by instituting mission-oriented research enterprises.

actors.name: Renaissance Humanists
actors.context: Cited as historical precedent for valuing prose style as a primary cultural aim.

actors.name: Graduate students in literature
actors.context: Cited as empirical evidence that literary knowledge does not produce writing skill.

actors.name: Writing Teachers
actors.context: Cited as having the good sense to abandon linguistics terminology in the classroom.

actors.name: University Colleagues
actors.context: Colleagues who feel misgivings about Hirsch's 'separatist' views on writing and logic.

actors.name: Frederick Crews
actors.context: Author of The Random House Handbook, cited for his maxims on tone and consistency.

actors.name: James McCrimmon
actors.context: Author of Writing with a Purpose, cited for his emphasis on outlining and paragraph unity.

actors.name: Ernest Gowers
actors.context: Author of The Complete Plain Words, cited for his emphasis on simplicity and clarity.

actors.name: Strunk
actors.context: Author of a standard composition handbook used for comparison.

actors.name: McCrimmon
actors.context: Author of a standard composition handbook used for comparison.

actors.name: Crews
actors.context: Author of a standard composition handbook used for comparison.

actors.name: Lucas
actors.context: Author of a standard composition handbook used for comparison.

actors.name: James McCrimmon
actors.context: Author of a writing handbook cited for typical stylistic maxims.

actors.name: Frederick Crews
actors.context: Author of a writing handbook cited for typical stylistic maxims.

actors.name: F.L. Lucas
actors.context: Author of a writing handbook cited for typical stylistic maxims.

actors.name: Writing Handbooks
actors.context: The author argues they overemphasize large-scale outlines at the expense of short-term integrative devices.

actors.name: Psychological researchers
actors.context: Cited as the source for the discovery that humans rapidly forget specific linguistic forms.

actors.name: Composition handbook writers
actors.context: Criticized for neglecting middle-range integrative devices due to a lack of coordinated research.

actors.name: Edward Sapir
actors.context: Linguist whose prose is analyzed for its readability and integrative devices.

actors.name: Anthony Burgess
actors.context: The subject of a student essay used as an example.

actors.name: Composition Teachers
actors.context: Characterized as often having unclear pedagogical purposes when marking papers.

actors.name: William Strunk Jr.
actors.context: The teacher of E.B. White, whose teaching style is scrutinized regarding the distinction between personal taste and objective craft.

actors.name: I. E. Fang
actors.context: Researcher who developed the 'Easy Listening Formula' used to validate the connection between readability and listenability.

actors.name: Frederick Crews
actors.context: Author of a popular composition handbook manual who advocates for pedagogical flexibility.

actors.name: College-level composition teachers
actors.context: The primary group whose pedagogical methods and challenges are discussed.

actors.name: Freshman composition instructors
actors.context: The group of practitioners who must decide whether to use textbooks and how to choose them.

actors.name: Textbook authors
actors.context: Authors of 'elephantine' and 'padded' composition manuals that lack authority.

actors.name: Composition Teachers
actors.context: Colleagues who use large manuals for 'intellectual respectability' but acknowledge their pedagogical downsides when challenged.

actors.name: Handbook Writers
actors.context: Authors of popular manuals who promote rigid formulas like the 'inverted funnel' for essays.

actors.name: Composition Researchers
actors.context: The group whose past research quality is described as low and whose future efforts need coordination.

actors.name: The Public
actors.context: The source of the 'outcry' over declining writing skills which provides the impetus for improved research status.

actors.name: Universities
actors.context: The institutions that have historically placed composition at the 'bottom of the heap' in terms of status.

actors.name: NCTE (National Council of Teachers of English)
actors.context: Identified as a teaching organization that lacks the scientific expertise to oversee rigorous experimental research.

actors.name: CCCC (Conference on College Composition and Communication)
actors.context: Identified as a teaching organization that lacks the scientific expertise to oversee rigorous experimental research.

actors.name: MLA (Modern Language Association)
actors.context: Identified as a teaching organization that lacks the scientific expertise to oversee rigorous experimental research, though noted for its new composition division.

actors.name: National Science Foundation
actors.context: Suggested as a model or source for the authoritative scientific guidance universities need to organize research missions.

actors.name: Modern Language Association
actors.context: Cited as an indicator of the rising status of composition through the creation of a dedicated division.

actors.name: Humanists
actors.context: The group traditionally tasked with solving composition problems, whom Hirsch argues cannot solve them alone.

objections_raised.objection: There is a necessary connection between the quality of writing and the quality of thought.
objections_raised.response: The author concedes that a connection often exists, but argues that teaching logic is a 'very inefficient way' to teach writing and that empirical research would likely show it is a waste of time.

objections_raised.objection: Composition should be a branch of rhetoric because writing involves audience awareness and persuasion.
objections_raised.response: Rhetoric is the closest fit, but it is not coextensive; many important genres (like technical manuals) are not rhetorical in the sense of being persuasive or emotive.

objections_raised.objection: The pedagogical discussion of maxims is less important than the theoretical parts of the book and offers nothing new to experienced teachers.
objections_raised.response: The discussion is included because it documents the theoretical case by showing how maxims correlate with psychological principles, and because it may be useful to some readers.

objections_raised.objection: Since the value of maxims depends on genre, should students simply be given different sets of maxims for every different kind of writing (essays, letters, stories)?
objections_raised.response: This might be effective, but it is insufficient; students must learn the underlying principles that explain why different rules apply to different genres.

objections_raised.objection: Should writing instruction dispense with maxims altogether and teach abstract principles instead?
objections_raised.response: No, because principles are too abstract for students to use directly. However, teachers should know the principles to apply maxims flexibly and accurately.

objections_raised.objection: Is there ever a defense for being a boring writer or having a monotonous style?
objections_raised.response: Only if the writer specifically and deliberately intends to be boring for a specific effect.

objections_raised.objection: Does shifting the design of a text necessarily violate the rules of good prose?
objections_raised.response: If the shift is deliberate, the reader must assume it was part of the original design to understand the intended effect of confusion.

objections_raised.objection: Middle-range integrative devices like proleptics and thematic tags are frequently neglected in standard composition handbooks.
objections_raised.response: This neglect stems from a historical lack of coordinated research into the psychology of composition, specifically the recent discovery that readers rapidly forget linguistic forms.

objections_raised.objection: The proposed maxims might not be the most efficient for actually teaching composition to students.
objections_raised.response: The author admits that factors in the learning process are complex and that his predictions are currently 'stabs in the dark' requiring further research.

objections_raised.objection: Red ink/comments on a paper are simply a justification or defense of the grade given.
objections_raised.response: Teachers should move red ink from the body of the paper to a summary paragraph focused on future performance.

objections_raised.objection: Composition instruction is merely catering to a teacher's personal whims and idiosyncratic tastes.
objections_raised.response: Teachers must use a tone that emphasizes writing as an objective craft to be learned, not a matter of subjective preference.

objections_raised.objection: Critiquing a student's prose style is an invasive tampering with their unique personality.
objections_raised.response: This is a 'tangled half-truth.' Writing is a craft with objective principles like readability, and teacher comments should be framed to emphasize this craft aspect rather than personal preference.

objections_raised.objection: Teaching the grapholect as a universal 'native' dialect is a violation of certain values (likely cultural or dialectal integrity).
objections_raised.source: Unspecified critics ('Those who scold the teacher of literacy')
objections_raised.response: These critics are attacking the values of literacy itself and increasing the emotional and professional burden on teachers who are performing a necessary task.

objections_raised.objection: If we lack definitive research on textbook formats and organization, should we wait for better data before designing or using them?
objections_raised.response: No, because we still need to teach and learn composition in the meantime, necessitating speculations based on current principles and practical experience.

objections_raised.objection: Why do teachers continue to use thick, 'elephantine' textbooks if they are pedagogically counterproductive?
objections_raised.response: The choice usually arises from a non-pedagogical desire for intellectual respectability in a course that lacks literary content, rather than from a belief in the manual's utility.

objections_raised.objection: Thick manuals provide a course with intellectual substance.
objections_raised.response: The 'intellectual respectability' provided by thickness is an outward sign that masks a lack of efficiency; pedagogical disadvantages outweigh the perceived status.

objections_raised.objection: Research is futile because writing quality continues to decline despite increased research.
objections_raised.response: While research cannot fix culture (TV) or motivation, it can still provide better tools for the classroom (implied in 'optimistic prediction').

objections_raised.objection: Research has continued to pile up while the quality of writing has continued to decline, suggesting research is ineffective.
objections_raised.response: TV culture makes efficient research more necessary because it must optimize the limited time students now spend on writing.

objections_raised.objection: Composition research has failed in the past, so why expect it to improve now?
objections_raised.response: Public outcry and the resulting rise in professional status will attract better talent and demand higher scientific standards.

objections_raised.objection: Teaching organizations like the NCTE and MLA should lead the charge in composition research because they represent the profession.
objections_raised.response: These are organizations of teachers, not scientific researchers; they lack competence in evaluating scientific quality or organizing multidisciplinary scientific missions.

I shall devote my final chapter to this problem [of evaluating the quality of writing].
